---
title: "33 · Gradient-TD: GTD, GTD2, and TDC"
subtitle: "What semi-gradient TD would be if it stopped cheating. Honest gradient on MSPBE — at the cost of a second parameter and a slower clock."
lesson-id: "mfrl.gradient-td"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.projected-bellman-operator", "mfrl.stochastic-gradient-descent", "mfrl.q-learning-with-fa"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 35
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you read on.

1. Can you write **MSPBE** in terms of $\Pi_\mu$, $T_\pi$, and $\hat v_w$
   from [Lesson 32](../32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd)?
2. Can you state the **semi-gradient TD** update and explain which gradient
   term it discards?
3. Are you comfortable with one step of [SGD](/glossary.qmd#stochastic-gradient-descent)
   on an expectation $\mathbb{E}[L(w, X)]$ using a single sample $X$?
4. Can you describe **Baird's counterexample** from
   [Lesson 30](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd) in one sentence?

If any answer is no, revisit the named lesson before continuing.
:::

## Touch

L29's [key insight](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd#discover)
was a confession: *the error is full; the gradient is half*. Semi-gradient
TD took the gradient of the prediction $\hat v(s; w)$, but pretended the
target $r + \gamma\, \hat v(s'; w)$ did not depend on $w$.

Take out two coloured pens. Sketch on paper:

- **Red:** the term semi-gradient TD already differentiates,
  $\nabla_w \hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)$.
- **Blue:** the term it threw away, $\gamma\,\nabla_w \hat v(s'; w) =
  \gamma\,\phi(s')$.

What does the *full* gradient of $\tfrac{1}{2}\delta^2$ look like if you
keep both pens?

$$\nabla_w \tfrac{1}{2}\delta^2 \;=\; \delta \cdot \bigl(\,\underbrace{-\phi(s)}_{\text{red}} \;+\; \underbrace{\gamma\,\phi(s')}_{\text{blue}}\,\bigr).$$

Adding blue back is what *residual gradient* did — L29 warned that it finds
the wrong fixed point in mis-specified models and learns slowly. Adding
blue is not the right fix.

The right fix is different. It says: stop trying to descend
$\tfrac12 \delta^2$ at all. **Descend MSPBE instead.**

::: {.lesson-beat}
Semi-gradient TD descends the right objective — MSPBE — but by an unsound
update (it ignores the target's $w$-dependence). Residual gradient
descends the wrong objective ($\tfrac12 \delta^2$) by a sound update.
Gradient-TD descends the right objective by a sound update. The price:
**a second parameter** and a slower clock.
:::

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. What does it mean to "take the gradient of MSPBE"? MSPBE involves the
   projection $\Pi_\mu$, which involves $\mu$ and $\Phi$ — but does it
   depend on $w$?
2. The gradient of MSPBE will turn out to be a *product of expectations*.
   Why is that a problem when you only have one sample per step?
3. If the bottleneck is a product of expectations, what could you keep
   around between time steps to break it apart?
4. Two parameters $w$ and $u$, two updates per step. What goes wrong if
   you update them at the same rate?

## Discover

Six problems. The first three set up the obstruction; the next three
break it.

### Problem 1 — MSPBE in expectation form

For linear FA with $\hat v_w = \Phi w$, the **MSPBE** from
[L32](../32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd#name) is

$$\mathrm{MSPBE}(w) \;=\; \|\Phi w - \Pi_\mu T_\pi \Phi w\|_\mu^2.$$

Use the matrix form of $\Pi_\mu$ from L32's Name beat to show that

$$\mathrm{MSPBE}(w) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]^\top \;\mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]^{-1}\; \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi],$$

where $\delta = r + \gamma\,\phi(s')^\top w - \phi(s)^\top w$ is the TD
error along a sample transition $(s, r, s')$, and expectations are over
$s \sim \mu$, transition $(s, r, s') \sim p(\cdot \mid s, \pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The Bellman residual is $T_\pi \Phi w - \Phi w$. In matrix form on samples,
that's $\mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]$ — a $d$-vector, the *expected TD
update direction*. The projected residual squared in $\|\cdot\|_\mu$
contracts this $d$-vector through the inverse Gram matrix
$\mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]^{-1}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $A = \Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi = \mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]$ and
$b_w = \Phi^\top D_\mu (T_\pi \Phi w - \Phi w) = \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]$
(the $w$-dependence is in $\delta$). Using
$\Pi_\mu = \Phi A^{-1} \Phi^\top D_\mu$:

$$\Pi_\mu (T_\pi \Phi w - \Phi w) \;=\; \Phi A^{-1} b_w.$$

Take the $\mu$-weighted norm of $\Pi_\mu(T_\pi \Phi w - \Phi w)$:

$$\|\Phi A^{-1} b_w\|_\mu^2
\;=\; (A^{-1} b_w)^\top \Phi^\top D_\mu \Phi (A^{-1} b_w)
\;=\; b_w^\top A^{-1} A A^{-1} b_w
\;=\; b_w^\top A^{-1} b_w.$$

So $\mathrm{MSPBE}(w) = \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]^\top
\mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]^{-1} \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]$ — a
weighted-norm form of the *expected TD update direction*.
</details>

### Problem 2 — the true gradient is a product

Take $\nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE}(w)$. You may use the fact that
$\mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]$ does not depend on $w$. Show that

$$\nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE}(w) \;=\; -2\,\mathbb{E}_\mu\bigl[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top\bigr]\;\mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]^{-1}\;\mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi].$$

(Here $\phi = \phi(s)$ and $\phi' = \phi(s')$.) Why is this an obstacle
to one-step SGD?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\delta$ depends on $w$ through $\delta = r + \gamma\,\phi'^\top w
- \phi^\top w$, so
$\nabla_w \delta = -(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')$. Apply the chain rule to
$b_w^\top A^{-1} b_w$, treating $A^{-1}$ as $w$-independent.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$\nabla_w b_w = \mathbb{E}_\mu[\nabla_w \delta\,\phi^\top]^\top
= -\mathbb{E}_\mu[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]^\top$. Apply the
matrix calculus identity $\nabla_w (b_w^\top M b_w) = 2 (\nabla_w b_w)
M b_w$ for symmetric $M$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
By the chain rule:

$$\nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE}(w) \;=\; 2 (\nabla_w b_w)\, A^{-1}\, b_w
\;=\; -2\,\mathbb{E}_\mu[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]\;A^{-1}\;\mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi].$$

(The sign comes from $\nabla_w \delta = -(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')$.)

This gradient is a **product of two expectations**: $\mathbb{E}_\mu[(\phi
- \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]$ on the left, and
$\mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]$ inside the $A^{-1}$ on the right. With one
sample, you'd need to estimate *both* expectations independently — the
**double-sampling problem**. If you used the same sample for both, the
products of stochastic terms would not be an unbiased estimate of the
product of expectations; in general $\mathbb{E}[XY] \ne \mathbb{E}[X]\,\mathbb{E}[Y]$.

So *plain* SGD on MSPBE is unimplementable without two independent samples
of the same transition — which off-policy data simply doesn't give you.
</details>

### Problem 3 — break the product with a second parameter

Introduce a new vector $u \in \mathbb{R}^d$ that *tracks*
$A^{-1} \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]$ — the right-hand expectation. Suppose
we update $u$ to minimise

$$L(u; w) \;=\; \mathbb{E}_\mu\bigl[(\delta - \phi^\top u)^2\bigr].$$

Show that the minimiser $u^\star$ of $L$ (for fixed $w$) is exactly
$A^{-1} \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Take $\nabla_u L = 0$ and solve.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\nabla_u L = -2 \mathbb{E}_\mu[(\delta - \phi^\top u)\,\phi] = 0$ gives

$$\mathbb{E}_\mu[\phi \phi^\top]\, u^\star = \mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi],
\qquad u^\star = A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}_\mu[\delta\,\phi].$$

So $u^\star$ is exactly the inner expectation we needed. The trick: keep
$u$ around between time steps, and update it slowly with SGD on $L(u; w)$.
Now the gradient of MSPBE factors as
$\nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE} \;\approx\; -2\,\mathbb{E}_\mu[(\phi - \gamma
\phi')\,\phi^\top]\, u$, which **is** estimable from one sample as
$-2 (\phi - \gamma \phi')\,(\phi^\top u)$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — the GTD2 update

Combine Problems 2 and 3. Write down the per-sample updates for $w$ and
$u$. Use step sizes $\alpha$ (for $w$) and $\beta$ (for $u$).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$w$ moves opposite the gradient of MSPBE (factor in step size, drop the $-2$).
$u$ moves opposite the gradient of $L(u; w)$ at the current sample.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**GTD2** per-sample update on $(s, r, s')$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta \;&\leftarrow\; r + \gamma\,\phi'^\top w - \phi^\top w \\
w \;&\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,(\phi^\top u) \\
u \;&\leftarrow\; u + \beta\,(\delta - \phi^\top u)\,\phi
\end{aligned}$$

The $w$-update has the same shape as semi-gradient TD — *TD-error-like
quantity times a feature vector* — but the "TD-error-like quantity" is now
$\phi^\top u$, not $\delta$, and the feature it multiplies is
$(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')$, not $\phi$.

(*GTD1* used a slightly different update; *GTD2* is the cleaner variant
introduced by Sutton, Maei et al. in their 2009 sequel paper.)
</details>

### Problem 5 — TDC: same fixed point, less variance

The GTD2 $w$-update can be re-written using $\delta$ explicitly. Show that
the equivalent algebraic form

$$w \;\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\delta\,\phi \;-\; \alpha\,\gamma\,\phi'\,(\phi^\top u)$$

has the same fixed point as GTD2 but typically much lower variance.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
At the fixed point, $\mathbb{E}[(\delta - \phi^\top u)\phi] = 0$, so
$\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi] = A u$. Substitute that into GTD2's $w$-update
and see what you get.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
GTD2 update: $w \leftarrow w + \alpha (\phi - \gamma \phi') (\phi^\top u)$.
Expand: $\alpha (\phi^\top u)\,\phi - \alpha\,\gamma\,(\phi^\top u)\,\phi'$.
On expectation this is $\alpha\,(A u) - \alpha\,\gamma\,\mathbb{E}[\phi'
\phi^\top]\,u$. At the fixed point, $A u = \mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$, so
the first term becomes $\alpha\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$ — the
semi-gradient TD direction. The second term is a correction.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**TDC** ("TD with gradient correction"):

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta \;&\leftarrow\; r + \gamma\,\phi'^\top w - \phi^\top w \\
w \;&\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\delta\,\phi \;-\; \alpha\,\gamma\,\phi'\,(\phi^\top u) \\
u \;&\leftarrow\; u + \beta\,(\delta - \phi^\top u)\,\phi
\end{aligned}$$

The first $w$-term is identical to **semi-gradient TD**. The second term
is the **correction** that makes the update an *honest* gradient of
MSPBE — paid for by carrying $u$.

In expectation: $\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi - \gamma\,\phi'\,(\phi^\top u)]
= \mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi] - \gamma\,\mathbb{E}[\phi'\,\phi^\top]\,u$, and
at the joint fixed point $A u = \mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$ this becomes
$A u - \gamma\,\mathbb{E}[\phi' \phi^\top] u = (A - \gamma\,\mathbb{E}[\phi'
\phi^\top])\,u$. Setting this to zero gives the same equation as GTD2's
fixed point.

**Why TDC has lower variance.** GTD2's update is $\alpha (\phi - \gamma
\phi')(\phi^\top u)$ — a product of two random vectors, each variance-y.
TDC replaces the first random vector with $\delta\,\phi$ (whose mean is
$A u$ at the fixed point, with smaller variance than $A u$ recomputed via
$\phi(\phi^\top u)$). The correction term then subtracts only the *bias*,
not the full estimator. In practice TDC needs fewer samples than GTD2 to
reach the same accuracy.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Baird's counterexample: divergence vs convergence

[Baird's 7-state counterexample](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd) is a
linear-FA MDP where semi-gradient TD with off-policy data **diverges**
geometrically: weights grow without bound. Without redoing the construction
here, argue (using Problems 4 and 5) why **TDC converges** on the same
MDP, given the GTD/TDC convergence theorem (Sutton, Maei et al., 2009):
under linear FA, off-policy data with bounded importance weights, and
two-time-scale step sizes ($\beta \to 0$ slower than $\alpha$),
$(w_t, u_t)$ converges almost surely to a fixed point of MSPBE.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The semi-gradient TD analysis of Baird's example shows that the expected
update is $\mathbb{E}[\text{update}] = -\alpha\,M w$ for an
**unstable** $M$ (some eigenvalues with positive real part). That makes
the iteration diverge.

The gradient-TD updates are designed so that the expected joint
$(w, u)$-update descends MSPBE. As a function of $w$, MSPBE is
**lower-bounded by zero**; as long as the gradient estimator is unbiased
in expectation, the iteration cannot diverge to infinity. Two-time-scale
analysis (Borkar 1997) then shows the slow timescale converges to a
fixed point of MSPBE — exactly the projected Bellman fixed point we
named in [L32](../32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd).

In short: **TDC converges on Baird's counterexample**, where semi-gradient
TD diverges. This is the headline result of the gradient-TD program. The
deadly triad is mathematically defused — at the price of $u$, $\beta$,
and slower wall-clock convergence.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The gradient-TD line was opened by **Sutton, Maei, Precup, Bhatnagar,
Silver, Szepesvári, and Wiewiora (2009)** with the paper "A convergent
$O(n)$ temporal-difference algorithm for off-policy learning with linear
function approximation" (NeurIPS). The original GTD update was clean but
slow; **Sutton, Maei et al. (2009b, ICML)** released GTD2 and TDC the
same year. Why didn't gradient-TD take over deep RL? Variance was the
main issue: $u$ is itself noisy, so the correction can swing wildly with
neural-network features. DQN's engineering (target networks, replay
buffers) gave more reliable practical performance, even without the
convergence guarantee. The road-not-taken stayed not-taken.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
Gradient-TD is **what semi-gradient TD would be if it stopped cheating**.
The "cheat" was treating the target as $w$-independent. The fix is not
to put the missing gradient back (that's residual gradient, which targets
the wrong objective). The fix is to descend MSPBE itself — and the cost
of that honesty is a second parameter $u$ tracking
$\mathbb{E}[\phi \phi^\top]^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$, and a slower
clock for $u$ than for $w$. **The deadly triad is mathematically defused;
the engineering trade-off is variance.**
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **[gradient-TD family](/glossary.qmd#gradient-td)** consists of
SGD-style algorithms that descend MSPBE by tracking a second parameter
$u \in \mathbb{R}^d$ on a *slower* timescale than the main weights $w$:

**GTD2** per-sample updates:

$$\begin{aligned}
w \;&\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,(\phi^\top u) \\
u \;&\leftarrow\; u + \beta\,(\delta - \phi^\top u)\,\phi
\end{aligned}$$

**[TDC](/glossary.qmd#tdc)** (TD with gradient correction) — equivalent
fixed point, lower variance:

$$\begin{aligned}
w \;&\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\delta\,\phi \;-\; \alpha\,\gamma\,\phi'\,(\phi^\top u) \\
u \;&\leftarrow\; u + \beta\,(\delta - \phi^\top u)\,\phi
\end{aligned}$$

Under linear FA, off-policy data with bounded importance weights, and
two-time-scale Robbins–Monro step sizes
($\alpha, \beta \to 0$ with $\beta / \alpha \to 0$), $(w_t, u_t)$
converges almost surely to a fixed point of MSPBE — the **projected
Bellman fixed point** of [L32](../32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd)
— even when semi-gradient TD diverges.

:::

In one mnemonic line:

> *Two parameters, two clocks: $w$ chases the projected Bellman point,
> $u$ tracks the gradient correction.*

## Apply

### Worked example — TDC on a 2-state off-policy chain

Two states $\{1, 2\}$. Target policy $\pi$ goes $1 \to 1$, $2 \to 1$
(absorbing at 1). Behaviour policy $b$ samples uniformly over the two
states. Reward $r = 0$, $\gamma = 0.9$. Features
$\phi(1) = (1, 0)^\top$, $\phi(2) = (1, 2)^\top$.

The on-policy stationary distribution of $\pi$ puts all mass on state 1,
so the true value function under $\pi$ is $v_\pi = (0, 0)^\top$
(absorbing at the zero-reward state). The TD fixed point of MSPBE under
$\mu_b = (\tfrac12, \tfrac12)$ is also $w^\star = (0, 0)$ in this
setup (the only $w$ in the feature span that gives $\hat v = 0$).

Initialise $w = (1, 1)^\top$, $u = (0, 0)^\top$, $\alpha = 0.1$,
$\beta = 0.01$ (so $\beta < \alpha$). Sample one transition $(s = 2,
r = 0, s' = 1)$ — behaviour visits state 2, target sends it to state 1.

1. $\delta = 0 + 0.9 \cdot \phi(1)^\top w - \phi(2)^\top w
   = 0.9 \cdot 1 - 3 = -2.1$.
2. **TDC $w$-update.** $\phi^\top u = 0$, so the correction term is 0
   on the first step. $w \leftarrow (1, 1) + 0.1 \cdot (-2.1) \cdot
   (1, 2) = (1 - 0.21,\, 1 - 0.42) = (0.79,\, 0.58)$.
3. **$u$-update.** $u \leftarrow (0, 0) + 0.01 \cdot (-2.1 - 0)\cdot
   (1, 2) = (-0.021,\, -0.042)$.

On the next step, $u \ne 0$, so the correction term becomes active and
pulls the $w$-update toward the projected Bellman direction.

Run this for 10 000 steps and you find $w_t \to (0, 0)$. Semi-gradient
TD on this off-policy data tends to drift instead — the on-policy
mismatch breaks the contraction (see
[L32 Stretch S1](../32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd#practice---stretch)).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Write the GTD2 update on a single transition $(s, r, s')$ with
features $\phi(s) = (1, s)^\top$, $\gamma = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.1$,
$\beta = 0.01$. Use $s = 1$, $r = 1$, $s' = 2$, initial $w = (0, 0)$,
$u = (0, 0)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute $\delta$ first; then both updates.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\delta = 1 + 0.5 \cdot \phi(2)^\top w - \phi(1)^\top w = 1 + 0 - 0 = 1$.
$\phi^\top u = 0$. $\phi - \gamma\,\phi' = (1, 1) - 0.5 \cdot (1, 2) =
(0.5, 0)$. **GTD2** $w$: stays $(0, 0)$ because $\phi^\top u = 0$. $u$:
$u \leftarrow (0, 0) + 0.01 \cdot (1 - 0) \cdot (1, 1) = (0.01, 0.01)$.

First step: $u$ moves, $w$ stays. The auxiliary parameter has to warm up
before it can correct the main update.
</details>

**W2.** Same setup as W1 but use **TDC** instead of GTD2.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
TDC's $w$-update has a semi-gradient TD piece plus a correction.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\delta = 1$, $\phi^\top u = 0$. $w$: $w \leftarrow (0, 0) + 0.1 \cdot 1
\cdot (1, 1) - 0.1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1, 2) \cdot 0 = (0.1, 0.1)$. The
correction term vanishes (because $\phi^\top u = 0$), so TDC reduces to
*semi-gradient TD* on the first step. $u$: same as W1, $(0.01, 0.01)$.

**Key contrast with GTD2:** TDC's first step moves $w$ immediately —
it doesn't have to wait for $u$ to warm up. That's part of why TDC is
faster in practice.
</details>

**W3.** Why does TDC update $u$ on the *fast* timescale and $w$ on the
*slow* timescale? (Some treatments invert this — flag what's
conventional.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The convergence proof requires $u$ to track $A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$
*as a function of $w$*. For that tracking to be accurate, $u$ must move
*faster* than $w$, so it equilibrates between $w$-updates.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Convention:** $u$ uses the larger step size $\beta$ (fast), $w$ uses
the smaller $\alpha$ (slow); typically $\beta / \alpha \to \infty$ as
$t \to \infty$, or equivalently $\alpha_t / \beta_t \to 0$.

The reason: the convergence proof uses **two-time-scale stochastic
approximation** (Borkar 1997). From $w$'s perspective, $u$ has already
converged to its $w$-dependent optimum $u^\star(w) = A^{-1}\,
\mathbb{E}[\delta(w)\,\phi]$; from $u$'s perspective, $w$ is frozen. That
separation requires $u$ to equilibrate faster than $w$ moves.

Some early gradient-TD papers swap the notation — the *unique* parameter
is "the slow one" in the convergence-proof sense, whichever symbol you
give it. Here we follow Sutton & Barto's convention: $\alpha$ on $w$,
$\beta$ on $u$, with $\beta > \alpha$. (Yes, that means the "slow" $w$
uses the smaller step size — confusing because "slow" feels like "small"
but in this context "slow" means "moves less per unit time," which is
exactly what a smaller step achieves.)
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Show that, *in expectation*, the GTD2 $w$-update equals
$-\tfrac12 \nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE}(w)$ when $u$ has reached its
$w$-dependent optimum $u^\star(w) = A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
From Problem 2's solution,
$\nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE} = -2\,\mathbb{E}[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]\,A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]
= -2\,\mathbb{E}[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]\,u^\star(w)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The GTD2 update is $\Delta w = \alpha\,(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,(\phi^\top u)$.
Take expectation and substitute $u = u^\star(w)$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\Delta w] = \alpha\,\mathbb{E}[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]\,u^\star(w)
= \alpha\,\mathbb{E}[(\phi - \gamma\,\phi')\,\phi^\top]\,A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$$
$$= -\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\,\nabla_w \mathrm{MSPBE}(w).$$

So GTD2 is **honest stochastic gradient descent on MSPBE** (modulo the
constant factor 2 and the assumption that $u$ has equilibrated). That is
precisely the convergence guarantee Problem 6 cited.
</details>

**P2.** Verify TDC reduces to *semi-gradient TD* when $u \equiv 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at TDC's $w$-update with $u = 0$ plugged in.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$w \leftarrow w + \alpha\,\delta\,\phi - \alpha\,\gamma\,\phi'\,(\phi^\top \cdot 0)
= w + \alpha\,\delta\,\phi$ — exactly the semi-gradient TD update from
L29. So TDC is "semi-gradient TD plus a correction the auxiliary
parameter learns." When $u$ is at its initial zero, TDC *is*
semi-gradient TD. As $u$ warms up the correction kicks in, and at the
fixed point the correction exactly cancels the semi-gradient bias that
would otherwise drag $w$ to the wrong place under off-policy data.
</details>

**P3.** A student says: "Gradient-TD only matters off-policy. On-policy,
semi-gradient TD is fine, so we should always use semi-gradient TD when we
can." Is this right?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
What does the deadly triad require for divergence to even be possible?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Half right.** On-policy linear FA semi-gradient TD converges
(Tsitsiklis–Van Roy 1997), and TDC's correction term equals zero in
expectation in that regime — so TDC offers no theoretical advantage
on-policy and gives up some speed. **In practice, when you know you're
on-policy with linear FA, use semi-gradient TD.**

But many practitioners aren't sure if they're on-policy. Experience
replay (DQN) breaks the on-policy property by definition. Off-policy
evaluation of one policy from another's trajectories (recommender
systems, medical RL) is off-policy by construction. In those settings,
TDC's convergence guarantee is meaningful. The cost (a second parameter,
slower convergence) is sometimes worth paying.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Emphatic TD** is a different fix for the deadly triad: instead
of carrying an auxiliary parameter, it *re-weights* the per-sample TD
update by a state-dependent factor $F_t$ that compensates for the
off-policy mismatch. Sketch (informally) why this might work, and state
one practical disadvantage relative to TDC.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The deadly triad arose because $\mu_b \ne \mu_\pi$. What if you could
construct sample weights that recovered $\mu_\pi$-norm contraction
behaviour?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Emphatic TD (ETD)** (Sutton, Mahmood & White, 2016) introduces a
recursive weight $F_t$ that essentially counts "how much would the target
policy have visited this state, given the trajectory so far?". Re-weighting
the standard TD update by $F_t$ restores a contraction property in the
*emphatic norm* — and ETD converges with linear FA off-policy.

**Practical disadvantage:** $F_t$ can have **very high variance** under
off-policy data, especially when the target and behaviour policies
differ a lot. Numerical instability is the usual symptom. TDC's $u$ is
also noisy but is bounded by the geometry of $\phi$; $F_t$ has no such
bound and can blow up.

Both ETD and TDC are correct convergent algorithms; both have variance
issues; neither has displaced the engineering DQN does. Sutton & Barto
§11.7 give a clean comparison.
</details>

**S2.** Convert the TDC update to the **off-policy** setting using
importance sampling. (Behaviour $b$ generates data, target $\pi$ is
evaluated. Per-step importance ratio
$\rho_t = \pi(a_t \mid s_t) / b(a_t \mid s_t)$.) Where does $\rho_t$ enter
the TDC update?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Every place an expectation under $\mu_\pi$ is being approximated by an
empirical average over $\mu_b$-data, weight the sample by $\rho_t$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Off-policy TDC:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_t \;&\leftarrow\; r_t + \gamma\,\phi_{t+1}^\top w - \phi_t^\top w \\
w \;&\leftarrow\; w + \alpha\,\rho_t\,\bigl[\delta_t\,\phi_t - \gamma\,\phi_{t+1}\,(\phi_t^\top u)\bigr] \\
u \;&\leftarrow\; u + \beta\,\rho_t\,(\delta_t - \phi_t^\top u)\,\phi_t
\end{aligned}$$

The importance ratio $\rho_t$ multiplies the *entire* per-sample update —
both for $w$ and for $u$ — to convert the $\mu_b$-empirical average into
an estimator of the $\mu_\pi$-expectation. That extra $\rho_t$ factor is
exactly where the variance creeps in: $\rho_t$ can be large when
$\pi(a \mid s) \gg b(a \mid s)$.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Gradient-TD is just TD with the missing gradient added back."** No.
   That's *residual gradient*, which targets $\tfrac12\,\delta^2$ — the
   *wrong* objective when $v_\pi \notin \mathcal{F}$. Gradient-TD targets
   MSPBE — the *right* objective. The two algorithms find different
   fixed points; one is the projected Bellman point, one is not.
2. **"$u$ is a value function."** It's not. $u$ has the same dimension as
   $w$ ($d$ features) and lives in feature-space, but it represents
   $A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$ — a "gradient correction." Calling
   it a value-function approximator is a category error.
3. **"Two-time-scale just means use different step sizes."** Stronger
   than that: the proof needs $\beta_t / \alpha_t \to \infty$
   asymptotically. In practice constant ratios like $\beta = 10 \alpha$
   work, but the asymptotic regime is what licenses the convergence
   theorem.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Gradient-TD descends MSPBE honestly.** Semi-gradient TD's "cheat"
   (ignore the target's $w$-dependence) is replaced by a real gradient
   estimator, paid for with a second parameter $u$ tracking
   $A^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$.
2. **TDC = semi-gradient TD + correction.** When $u = 0$ TDC *is*
   semi-gradient TD. As $u$ warms up the correction cancels the
   off-policy bias. At the fixed point, the corrected update has
   expectation zero — MSPBE is at a minimum.
3. **The deadly triad is defused, but variance is not.** TDC converges
   where semi-gradient TD diverges (Baird's counterexample). Why didn't
   it take over? Variance from $u$ is the practical cost, and DQN's
   engineering tricks dominate when the workload is forgiving.

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you write down TDC's two updates from memory?
2. Did you verify in Problem 5 that TDC and GTD2 have the same fixed
   point but different per-sample variance?
3. Could you explain to a friend why "ignore the target's gradient" was
   wrong, and why "put the target's gradient back" was *also* wrong?

- **3 yes:** continue to
  [Lesson 34 — Policy Parameterisation](../34-policy-parameterization/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo Problem 5, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** re-derive Problem 2 and Problem 4 by hand.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.projected-bellman-operator](../32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd)** —
  MSPBE is the objective; the projected Bellman fixed point is the
  thing TDC converges to.
- **[mfrl.stochastic-gradient-descent](../22-stochastic-gradient-descent/index.qmd)** —
  every per-sample update here is SGD on an expectation, with the
  Robbins–Monro step-size conditions in the background.
- **[mfrl.q-learning-with-fa](../30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)** —
  the lesson where divergence first appeared; gradient-TD is the
  alternative that didn't take that route.

### What this unlocks

Nothing else in this track depends on gradient-TD. This is the
**road-not-taken** branch of the FA arc — the mathematically clean answer
to the deadly triad, which the rest of modern deep RL declined in favour
of DQN's engineering. The next lesson resumes the main thread with
[policy parameterisation](../34-policy-parameterization/index.qmd) and
the actor-critic family.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Off-policy policy evaluation under linear FA is a live
research area — recommender-system A/B-testing, off-policy medical RL,
counterfactual reasoning in causal inference. In those settings divergence
is not an acceptable failure mode, and the gradient-TD family (along
with emphatic TD and recent gradient-Q variants) are the convergent
options.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Modern off-policy actor-critic (DDPG, SAC, TD3) descends
neither MSPBE nor MSTDE cleanly — they use semi-gradient updates and
trust engineering (target networks, twin critics, clipped double Q) to
suppress the divergence the gradient-TD family solves theoretically.
Understanding *what* the engineering is suppressing is the value of this
lesson.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
mentions gradient-TD as the "convergent alternative to DQN" without
deriving it. This lesson is the derivation.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §8.4 (gradient-TD methods); also Sutton, Maei et al.
  NeurIPS 2009 (GTD), ICML 2009 (GTD2, TDC); Sutton & Barto, *RL: An
  Introduction* (2nd ed.), §11.7.
- The "stop cheating" framing (key-insight) is borrowed from Sutton's own
  exposition in the 2009 papers — he uses the words "the algorithm we
  presented previously is *not* a true stochastic gradient method."
- Touchstones honoured: Russian problem-first sequencing (Problems 1–6
  derive GTD2 and TDC before they're named); Better Explained "Aha!"
  framing (semi-gradient = cheat; gradient-TD = honest); two-coloured-pen
  Touch builds the residual-vs-projection distinction physically before
  it's named.
:::
